full_response:
Below we work in units with c=1 and define  
r*≡∫dr/(1–2GM/r)  (the “tortoise” coordinate), so that in (t,r*)–coordinates the Schwarzschild line element (t–r sector) is  
 ds² = –(1–2GM/r) dt² + (1–2GM/r) dr*².  

Define light‐cone coordinates  
 u = t – r*, v = t + r*.  
Then  
 du = dt – dr*, dv = dt + dr*,  
and hence we can solve  
 dt = (du + dv)/2, dr* = (dv – du)/2.  

Substitute into ds²:

ds² = –(1–2GM/r) (dt)² + (1–2GM/r) (dr*)²  
     = –(1–2GM/r)·[(du+dv)/2]² + (1–2GM/r)·[(dv–du)/2]²  

Expand:

–(1–2GM/r)·(du² + 2 du dv + dv²)/4  
+ (1–2GM/r)·(dv² – 2 du dv + du²)/4  

The du² and dv² terms cancel, leaving

ds² = –(1–2GM/r)·(2 du dv)/4 – (1–2GM/r)·(2 du dv)/4  
     = –(1–2GM/r) du dv.  

Thus in (u,v) coordinates the t–r part of the metric is

 ds² = –F(r(u,v)) du dv, where F(r)=1–2GM/r  

and r is understood as a function of (u,v) via inversion of u=t–r*, v=t+r*.  

Reading off the metric components:

 g_{uu} = 0, g_{vv} = 0,  
 g_{uv} = g_{vu} = –½ F(r(u,v)) = –½[1–2GM/r(u,v)].  

Boxing the requested result:

\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
ds^2_{(t,r)}  &= -\bigl(1-\tfrac{2GM}{r(u,v)}\bigr)\,du\,dv,\\
g_{uu}&=0,\quad g_{vv}=0,\quad g_{uv}=g_{vu}=-\tfrac12\Bigl(1-\tfrac{2GM}{r(u,v)}\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
}

Confidence: 96%
